introSection: Python was a roller coaster manufactured by Arrow Development that was formerly located at Adventure World in Upper Marlboro, Maryland, USA. It originally operated at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey, USA from 1978 to 1992 as the upper coaster of Lightnin' Loops.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
Lightnin' Loops opened at Six Flags Great Adventure in 1978. The attraction consisted of two separate roller coasters with interlocking loops, with an upper loop (this article) and a lower loop.

The ride temporarily closed after an incident occurred on it on June 17, 1987. It reopened on October 10, 1987, with modifications made to the restraints to prevent a similar accident. The lower loop also closed after the incident, but it did not reopen at Six Flags Great Adventure and remained standing but not operating.

The upper loop closed in 1992 and both coasters were removed from the park to make room for Batman The Ride. They were sold to Tierco Group, Inc., which separated the attractions. The upper loop was relocated to Adventure World, where it was modified and reopened in 1993 as Python. The lower loop was relocated to Frontier City in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, USA, where it reopened in 1994 as Diamond Back.

Python operated through 1998 before being removed as part of the park's conversion into Six Flags America in 1999. It was replaced by Two-Face: The Flip Side. It was put into storage before being scrapped in 2005.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Blue track and supports. The ride used to feature yellow track with black rails and yellow supports. It had white track with blue rails and white supports at Great Adventure.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content:
On June 17, 1987, 19-year-old Karen Brown was killed after falling from the ride. An investigation by the State Labor Department concluded that the ride itself was operating properly, but that the ride operator started the ride without having made sure that all of the passengers were secured by the safety harnesses. The Department's Office of Safety Compliance further concluded that the accident would not have occurred had proper procedures been followed.

The park was found to be in violation of the Carnival/Amusement Ride Safety Act and was subsequently charged with the maximum state fines of $1,000. The ride was reopened on Saturday, October 10, 1987, with modifications made to the restraints to prevent a similar accident.

fullText:
Python was a roller coaster manufactured by Arrow Development that was formerly located at Adventure World in Upper Marlboro, Maryland, USA. It originally operated at Six Flags Great Adventure in Jackson, New Jersey, USA from 1978 to 1992 as the upper coaster of Lightnin' Loops.

Lightnin' Loops opened at Six Flags Great Adventure in 1978. The attraction consisted of two separate roller coasters with interlocking loops, with an upper loop (this article) and a lower loop.

The ride temporarily closed after an incident occurred on it on June 17, 1987. It reopened on October 10, 1987, with modifications made to the restraints to prevent a similar accident. The lower loop also closed after the incident, but it did not reopen at Six Flags Great Adventure and remained standing but not operating.

The upper loop closed in 1992 and both coasters were removed from the park to make room for Batman The Ride. They were sold to Tierco Group, Inc., which separated the attractions. The upper loop was relocated to Adventure World, where it was modified and reopened in 1993 as Python. The lower loop was relocated to Frontier City in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, USA, where it reopened in 1994 as Diamond Back.

Python operated through 1998 before being removed as part of the park's conversion into Six Flags America in 1999. It was replaced by Two-Face: The Flip Side. It was put into storage before being scrapped in 2005.



Blue track and supports. The ride used to feature yellow track with black rails and yellow supports. It had white track with blue rails and white supports at Great Adventure.



On June 17, 1987, 19-year-old Karen Brown was killed after falling from the ride. An investigation by the State Labor Department concluded that the ride itself was operating properly, but that the ride operator started the ride without having made sure that all of the passengers were secured by the safety harnesses. The Department's Office of Safety Compliance further concluded that the accident would not have occurred had proper procedures been followed.

The park was found to be in violation of the Carnival/Amusement Ride Safety Act and was subsequently charged with the maximum state fines of $1,000. The ride was reopened on Saturday, October 10, 1987, with modifications made to the restraints to prevent a similar accident.
